perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of the Development Work HealthGate and the Publishers shall run acceptance tests to assure compliance with the Specification. Load testing will be conducted at HealthGate. Such period of acceptance testing shall not exceed 2 weeks from date of delivery for testing.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon completion of the Development Work HealthGate and the Publishers shall run validation tests to assure compliance with the Specification. Load testing will be conducted at HealthGate. Such period of acceptance testing shall not exceed 2 weeks from date of delivery for testing.
perturbation.explanation: The term **'acceptance tests'** is being replaced with **'validation tests.'** While these terms could conceptually overlap, within the contract **'Acceptance'** is specifically defined in Section 1, tying it to Clause 9. The change introduces uncertainty as it is not clear if **'validation tests'** are synonymous with **'acceptance tests'** and therefore have the same implications under Clause 9 or if it is a seperate action.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon passing the acceptance tests, the System shall be deemed Accepted
perturbation.justification: The original text used the term 'acceptance tests' which is explicitly defined and tied to Clause 9, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. By changing 'acceptance tests' to 'validation tests', the changed text introduces ambiguity because it is unclear whether 'validation tests' are equivalent to 'acceptance tests' and subject to the same conditions under Clause 9, as the contradicted text requires: 'Upon passing the acceptance tests, the System shall be deemed Accepted'. This creates a direct conflict, making it a strong in-text contradiction according to the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: HealthGate hereby grants to the Publishers a non-exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Proprietary Software for the purposes of this Agreement Save in relation to the Publishers' logos, trademarks, and content, HealthGate may use and/or licence the Proprietary Software for itself or for others without any compensation or liability to the Publishers. All Proprietary Software and Source Code remain the property of HealthGate. Publishers may not use either Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a product that competes with those services offered by HealthGate. HealthGate, in its sole discretion, retains the right to determine if Publishers are utilizing either the Proprietary Software or Source Code in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate hereby grants to the Publishers a non-exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Proprietary Software for the purposes of this Agreement Save in relation to the Publishers' logos, trademarks, and content, HealthGate may use and/or licence the Proprietary Software for itself or for others without any compensation or liability to the Publishers. All Proprietary Software and Source Code remain the property of HealthGate. Publishers may use the Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a competing product to HealthGate’s services. HealthGate, in its sole discretion, retains the right to determine if Publishers are utilizing either the Proprietary Software or Source Code in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Original texts states **'Publishers may not use either Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a product that competes with those services offered by HealthGate.'** Modified text, **'Publishers may use the Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a competing product to HealthGate’s services.'** This completely contradicts the original constraint on Publisher, now allowing them to develop similar services.
perturbation.contradicted_text: HealthGate hereby grants to the Publishers a non-exclusive non-transferable licence to use the Proprietary Software for the purposes of this Agreement Save in relation to the Publishers' logos, trademarks, and content, HealthGate may use and/or licence the Proprietary Software for itself or for others without any compensation or liability to the Publishers. All Proprietary Software and Source Code remain the property of HealthGate. Publishers may use the Proprietary Software or Source Code held in escrow to develop a competing product to HealthGate’s services. HealthGate, in its sole discretion, retains the right to determine if Publishers are utilizing either the Proprietary Software or Source Code in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibits Publishers from using Proprietary Software or Source Code to develop competing products. The changed text explicitly permits Publishers to do so. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, and therefore the contradiction exists in the changed text, between the original restriction, and the newly permitted clause. This creates a direct contradiction regarding whether or not Publishers are allowed to develop competing products, leading to potential legal disputes. The definition of misaligned terminology is satisfied in this case because the term usage is not being used interchangably but it is a contradictory statment, or obligation within the document.

perturbation.original_text: If the Publishers exercise their right of renewal under Clause 17.1, then the Publishers shall have a further right of renewal for each of the subsequent three years, provided that the right to renew shall be conditional upon the Publishers having exercised their right in the previous year, and giving notice on or before the 30 September before the renewal is to take effect.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Publishers exercise their right of renewal under Clause 17.1, then the Publishers shall have a further right of renewal for each of the subsequent three years, provided that the right to renew shall be conditional upon the Publishers having exercised their right in the previous year, and giving notice on or before the 30 June before the renewal is to take effect.
perturbation.explanation: The date for the right of renewal has been modified from **'30 September'** to **'30 June'**. This modification in the right of renewal introduces a contradiction, it is not clear when is the deadline to exercesi the right of renewal, on September or June.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Publishers shall have the right to renew the term of the Services by notice in writing to HealthGate to be given on or before 30 September 1999. If the Publishers exercise their right to renew, the term of the Services shall be extended by one further year, up to and including 28 February 2001. The Use Fees shall remain the same as in the Initial Period and the fee for the Services shall not exceed $7000 for additional journals, $2000 maintenance fee on existing journals and $2000 per Gigabyte.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the deadline for exercising the right of renewal from '30 September' to '30 June'. The contradicted text states the renewal notice must be given on or before '30 September'. This creates a direct inconsistency regarding the deadline, leading to confusion and a potential dispute. A reasonable reader would immediately recognize the sections as incompatible because they provide different dates for the same action.
